channel.title: Today's Darling
channel.description: Daily essays by Shigesato Itoi from 1101.com, translated to English.

item.title: "Nobody special."
item.description: The essay argues that accepting yourself as fundamentally "nothing special" is liberating rather than limiting, allowing you to live authentically without the burden of pretense while still achieving joy, accomplishment, and meaningful experiences. It suggests that recognizing our universal ordinariness frees us from the exhausting need to prove our worth or appear exceptional.
item.content:encoded:
Being truly "nothing special." Being "nothing special" from the bottom of your heart. Your foolishness plain for everyone to see, everyone knowing full well you're "nothing special." You can try your best to bluff. You can put on the grandest airs you've got. But "nothing special" is "nothing special."

You might be making your own quiet efforts, struggling in ways nobody ever notices, but "nothing special" is still "nothing special." You might have a little kindness in you, you might manage a good deed now and then, but "nothing special" is, no matter what, "nothing special."

Win a gold medal, still "nothing special." Get called a great person, still "nothing special." Whatever you do, whatever you're capable of, you were "nothing special" to begin with, so "nothing special" you remain.

Me, you, him, her, all of us, "nothing special" from the day we were born. Once in a while we're a little "better." Once in a while we get a little "serious." But being "nothing special" doesn't mean you lose anything, doesn't put you at any disadvantage.

As "nothing special," you can still climb the highest mountains, dive into the deepest seas. You can stay "nothing special" and still laugh, cry, feel joy, find happiness, even pull off something incredible.

"Nothing special," and no complaints about it.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. There probably isn't a single person alive who is anything other than "nothing special."
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: I took notes outside the movie theater.
item.description: Watching an extraordinary film reminded the author that life's quality depends on the care and integrity we bring to it—much like how persistent, patient effort eventually creates profound impact, the small choices we make daily shape our entire existence.
item.content:encoded:
You know how raindrops, falling one by one onto a stone, can eventually wear a hole right through it? It really does happen. And even the tiniest droplet of a thought, if you keep thinking it over and over, might eventually become a word, like a hollow carved into stone.

Yesterday I went to see the Spanish film *Cerrar los ojos*, and, well, I'll try not to say much about the content, but it was extraordinary. The images just kept washing over me, one after another. The screenplay, the music and sound design, the direction, the acting, the cinematography, the world they built around it all. Every element felt flawless. You might even call it a perfectly played straight face, so committed there's nothing to challenge. It could be read as a grand tragicomedy, or something supremely cerebral, or something with a realism so vivid you'd mistake it for a documentary.

Starting from the point of "this is the film I want to make" and arriving at "it's finished!" in reality. The sheer toughness it must have taken, mentally, physically, financially, temporally, practically knocked me flat.

Seeing this incredible film must have converged with something I'd been feeling for a long time, like those steady, patient raindrops. The word "LIFE," and all the goodness in it, rose up again, towering before me.

I don't want to state these as arguments or positions. I want to write them as short, poem-like words, something I can set down right in front of me.

Don't cut corners on things. Cutting corners is cutting corners on your life.

A single day's living is a miniature of a whole life's living. What you seek today is what you seek for your entire life.

Cheap, fast, and in bulk? If you want to live a cheap, fast, bulk-produced life, be my guest.

I was scribbling notes like these as I walked out of the theater. The area around Shibuya Station was packed with countless lives, all jostling together. Everyone, living however they please. And that's fine.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. LIFE: it means life, living, livelihood, the raw and immediate, the whole of one's days. It matters.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: I had ramen again today, didn't I.
item.description: The author celebrates ramen both as a competitive craft worthy of deep expertise and study, while also appreciating the simple pleasure of enjoying good ramen without pretension or specialized knowledge.
item.content:encoded:
I'm a pretty big fan of ramen. First of all, I enjoy ramen as information. If there's an article about ramen, I'll read it. If the opportunity comes up, I'll visit shops here and there to eat. I have my favorites that I return to, of course, but if some new place catches my attention, I'll make the trip. I watch that BS-TBS show called "Eating Ramen." I have friends who are far more serious about ramen than I am, and I enjoy listening to them talk about it. I love eating the ramen itself, but learning about that whole world is fascinating too.

I think ramen fans, myself included, tend to look at the current ramen industry the way you'd watch a competitive sport. The playing field is fixed: one bowl. The time it takes to eat, the price per customer, the general ingredients, all more or less set. Noodles, broth, toppings. Those are the elements you compete with. You can put in as much effort as you want, source your ingredients as carefully as you like, go as far as your freedom takes you. If someone wants to pour their life into it, that's fine. What matters is whether the resulting bowl wins over the customer. Value equals victory, and the results show up in the lines, the reputation, the sales.

As knowledge and information about ramen accumulate, you get more and more so-called connoisseurs with an unreasonable depth of expertise. That's another way it resembles competitive sports. Sometimes a customer ends up opening their own ramen shop. Just as strategy and tactics in sports become increasingly specialized, the methods for making broth grow into something like arcane secret arts. Some of it can be understood through logic and science. Some of it sounds more like the work of an alchemist. Either way is fine, as long as it produces something of value. Ramen as reading material is thrilling and fascinating.

All of that is how I genuinely feel, but then again... a simple "ramen" sitting on the menu at a good neighborhood Chinese joint, that's delicious too. Something that doesn't seem to involve any secret techniques at all. If it tastes good, it wins. Being just a regular ramen-loving customer is a pretty carefree deal. Sorry about that.

Thanks for visiting Hobonichi again today. I'm probably on the not-very-knowledgeable end of the ramen-lover spectrum.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Cowboys as workers.
item.description: The essay reflects on how the simple privilege of choosing what to eat stems from the sacrifices of pioneers like cowboys who endured monotonous, grueling labor, reminding us that modern comfort rests on a solid historical foundation of hardship and sacrifice.
item.content:encoded:
Every day, thinking "What should I eat today?" Some people find that tedious, but really, it's a blessing. Just having choices is no small thing.

Years ago, there was an American TV series called *Rawhide*, back when Clint Eastwood was still a young up-and-comer. It was the story of cowboys driving cattle across the country, and every single day, day after day, their meal was the same: beans. I think it was salt pork and kidney beans stewed together. The cowboys would grumble, "Beans again? Every damn day," and then old Wishbone, the cook, would fire back with his usual line: "Don't like it, don't eat it!" I used to watch that exchange as a little kid and think it was hilarious. That bit, like a comedy routine, stuck with me all these years. I still think about it often, even as a grown man. What stayed with me is the hardship of eating the same thing every single day.

"Cowboy" literally means someone who drives cows. They were laborers, moving thousands of head of cattle for a living. Not the handsome types in ten-gallon hats twirling pistols and lassoing outlaws. These were men on horseback in rain and wind, driving herds day after day. And eating nothing but beans while they did it. People like us today couldn't handle that. It was brutal. The same went for the transcontinental railroad of that era. The relatively short history of the vast United States is, in many ways, a story of grueling, backbreaking pioneering.

I know this is starting to sound preachy, but being able to say "What should I eat today?" is something that rests on the mud-caked story of those people who drove cattle across the land and ate nothing but beans. Hey, are you listening? I'm talking to you, me.

I don't know. There's a kind of respect for that history that we shouldn't let ourselves forget. That's what I find myself thinking, even now, late as it is. No matter how much information piles up, no matter how advanced AI becomes, without a real, solid foundation underneath, it's all just clouds. Somebody has to provide the water, the beans, the cattle, the roads.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi today, too. If America's history is one of frontier and pioneering, I wonder what kind of history Japan's would be.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: There is no dog that doesn't play at all.
item.description: The author reflects on how their current low-energy dog Buiko, though less demanding than their previous high-spirited dog Bouillon, still seeks out play in her own subtle way, revealing that play remains essential to life for dogs and humans alike. Despite their different temperaments and energy levels, both dogs demonstrate that the desire to play is a fundamental part of their nature.
item.content:encoded:
Our dog Buiko is, for a dog, not very active. Don't get me wrong, she's adorable, but active she is not. She seems to like going outside, yet she's not fond of walks. She'll wander around the yard for a bit, then immediately take on an attitude of "Shall we go back in now?" Our previous dog, Bouillon, never once said "That's enough" no matter how long a walk lasted. It was always "More!" On top of that, even when she was inside, she was constantly bouncing around, looking for any chance to play. She'd grab a ball, trot over, and drop it right at my feet, demanding I throw it. (Iwata-san got roped into that one plenty of times.) When you threw it, she'd race after it, sprint back at full speed, and drop it again. Over and over, as many times as you'd let her. She was a dog who lived for play, for whom physical activity was the greatest joy. With the energy levels we have now, there's no way we could keep up with her.

It's not as though we chose our match based on that kind of convenience, but the energy-saving lifestyle of our second dog has been a real blessing. The only time she gets active is when she's "reporting for duty" to announce that someone has come to the front door. That's the one occasion when she barks her heart out. You always do good work, Buiko. Thank you.

Now, even a dog like our second one will, every once in a while, come over to invite me to play. She'll position herself in my line of sight and let out a quiet little grumble. When I respond to the signal and stand up, she runs ahead to where the toys are kept, picks up a rubber ball, and sets it down on the spot. I throw it and she goes after it, but she doesn't bring it back. She just stays where she is, waiting for me to walk over to her. There's the "go" of back-and-forth, but no "back." So I have no choice but to go to her. The dog simply waits. From wherever she's standing, I pick up the ball and throw it again. She chases it, then stops where it lands. After a few rounds of this, she stops chasing the ball altogether. A dog, not chasing a ball. And that's the end of it, just a few minutes in all.

But the interesting thing is, it's not that she doesn't want to play. Well, say what you will, I suppose the dog in her blood is still there after all.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi today. Bouillon, Buiko, and I: none of us could go through life without play.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: A world where you can find almost anything if you look
item.description: The essay argues that universal agreement is impossible and that modern technology's ability to expose us to countless diverse—and sometimes hurtful—opinions can disturb our mental equilibrium, making it important to resist both ego-searching and the compulsion to know everything about what others think.
item.content:encoded:
It's never the case that all one hundred people out of a hundred are thinking the same thing. Even something you feel sure about, if half the people in the world happen to agree with you, you can safely say, "Well, that's a pretty good number." You might be absolutely convinced that "when it comes to oden, it's all about the daikon," but how many people would actually nod along and say, "Yes, daikon"? And the same goes for eggs. Konjac might be practically synonymous with oden, but I don't think all that many people would name it as their personal number one. (Based on a highly informal neighborhood survey.) When it comes to the question "What do you think of when you think of oden?", there is simply no answer that everyone will unanimously agree on.

So if you go around brimming with confidence, convinced that "everyone loves eggs," and you actually put the question to people, you'll find yourself caught off guard by voices calling out "shirataki!" or "chikuwa!" Someone with a narrow mind might even get angry. "How outrageous!"

I keep saying this, but we really need to understand that it's absolutely impossible for a hundred people to all be thinking the same thing. You can't just declare, "Everyone must want this kind of world," or "This is how people think." Outlandish opinions, ideas you'd never expect, for better or worse, they absolutely exist out there in the world.

People often say that ego-searching, looking yourself up online, is bad for your mental health, and you should stop doing it. That's because it's essentially going out of your way to hunt for information that will hurt you. If you make the effort to poke around in every back alley and side street, you will always find what's already there, because it's always been there. And once you notice it, it can throw off your inner sense of balance. Getting swept up in a whirlpool of agreement and affirmation is dangerous too, but so is fixating on every dark stain on the wall.

We live in an age where you can go and see the thoughts of not just a hundred people, but a million, as many as you like, whenever you want. That might actually be a strange thing for human beings. Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. Knowing too much, wanting to know too much. It pays to be careful.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Something like the acknowledgments at the end of a book's afterword.
item.description: The author reflects on the successful completion of the "Life is Fun" exhibition, celebrating the seamless teamwork, joy, and collaborative spirit demonstrated by everyone involved—from crew and staff to vendors, volunteers, and visitors. Through heartfelt gratitude, they emphasize how the collective effort and genuine enthusiasm of all participants made the event a meaningful and joyful experience.
item.content:encoded:
I know I keep writing about the same thing every day, but the truth is, where we are and what we're thinking about has been nothing but the "Life is Fun" exhibition, so forgive me. One week starting June 1st, or ten days if you count setup and construction. We made it to the finish line: "Safety first, fun second." It was fun, wasn't it? Wasn't it fun for you, too?

As usual, I'm going to sing the praises of my people. The crew and the shop staff were all marveling, "I had no idea we'd gotten this strong." But honestly, come on, it's because every one of you thought things through, made changes, and gave it your all this time around. That's what made everything run so smoothly. Even during teardown and the after-party, smiles were blooming everywhere. Even when people were working at incredible speed, there was no shouting, no barking orders. Everyone just moved naturally. I have so much respect for everyone who worked here. You all looked great. More than anything, you looked like you were having fun. When I asked, almost like a greeting, "Did you have fun?" everyone answered instantly, "We had fun!" That made me happy too.

To all the vendors: thank you for boarding this ship with us. And to the part-timers: you were truly wonderful. People who've been with us every time since the first one in Roppongi. Parents and children working together. People who came from far away. People who made friends here. People from Hobonichi's business partners. People who might even be older than me. Former Hobonichi crew members. Mothers who waited for their children to grow up and could finally join. So many of you are already putting in reservations, saying, "I want to do this again next time."

Enormous, enormous gratitude to the families, friends, and neighbors who cheered on the crew working here. To our "landlords" at Sumitomo Realty, to everyone who handled construction, logistics, and security, to Aki Kondo, to Tobi who performed every single day: thank you! And to the Hobonichi Techo team, who held down the fort at our Kanda headquarters the whole time: thank you. And finally, to all the many customers who came and took part: thank you!

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi today, too. This is Itoi, who got home, lay down on the sofa, and immediately got a leg cramp.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Alright, year 29 has begun.
item.description: Hobonichi's 29 years of success came not from having a clear corporate strategy, but from cultivating an open, creative environment where ideas naturally grow and evolve, nurturing both the company and the people within it. The company's philosophy prioritizes the beauty of the process itself over predetermined goals, allowing for unexpected discoveries and genuine human connection.
item.content:encoded:
As of today, Hobonichi has entered its 29th year. Yesterday, June 6th, was the 28th anniversary of our founding, which means the very next day we're already into year 29. I wonder if everyone busy working at the "Life's Little Pleasures Exhibition" even knows. Hey, year 29 has begun!

But really, if someone asked, "What kind of company is Hobonichi?" I'm not sure even we could give a clear answer. Isn't that a problem, though? You're supposed to know what you're doing so you can buckle down and do it. You're supposed to have a goal so you can work backward and figure out what needs doing now. Nobody ever really nagged us about that kind of thing, though. Maybe they figured there was no point. But even without being told, we've managed just fine.

Seeds get planted in an "open lot of ideas" that isn't the same as everyone else's. Those seeds, through sun and clouds and rain, sometimes grow tall and sometimes don't. The leaves photosynthesize, flowers bloom, roots drink up water and nutrients from deep underground, and if things go well, fruit appears. But if you fixate too much on the fruit as the purpose, you end up chasing nothing but that. What matters more is the process, wondering what will grow, noticing how beautiful it is, finding it fascinating. That process is what nurtures us, the ones doing the nurturing.

And having been nurtured, we go out with energy to find another "open lot of ideas" and plant more seeds. Maybe a field takes shape. Maybe a forest. It's not just us, the people, who grow. The place itself grows. Bees come buzzing, birds arrive, animals wander in. Follow a rabbit and you might find a mysterious hole. It's wonderful. And because it's wonderful, you can play with everything you've got. You end up completely exhausted, and you look around at your companions and think, I'm so glad they're here.

Well then, today is the final day of the "Life's Little Pleasures Exhibition." Thank you, as always, for being here with Hobonichi. I think AI is amazing, but I'd like to believe that people are even more so.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: There isn't that much I can answer
item.description: Not all feelings can be immediately understood or articulated, and the gradual process of coming to comprehend our emotions and relationships over time is what gives life its meaning. Rather than viewing uncertainty as a failure, we should accept that ambiguity and incomplete understanding are natural and valuable parts of being human.
item.content:encoded:
When I was a child, when I was young, adults would ask me, "What do you want to be when you grow up?" and the truth was, I had no idea. When my friends talked about which singers or actors on TV or in the movies they liked best, I didn't know that either. I felt like I had to pick something, had to give an answer. The truth was I never really knew, but I'd think it over and somehow settle on something. It was always a forced decision. The fact is, not every question has a ready answer. That's true for children, and it's just as true for adults.

You can't have a reaction to everything, put every single thing into words. It's not a matter of failing to "verbalize" your feelings. Half the time, there's simply no reaction there to begin with. If someone told me I was lacking in sensitivity, well, maybe so. If someone asked whether I was feeling anything at all, the honest answer would be: maybe not, maybe so.

There are ways of feeling that you don't even notice yourself, and sometimes it takes years, a very long time, before you finally understand what they were. This happens all the time with relationships. The people closest to you, your father, your mother. What I actually felt about them, what I actually thought, I don't think I ever really understood. Love, dislike, gratitude, resentment. Honestly, I couldn't sort out any of those feelings.

People can't just answer whatever they're asked. Does anyone actually exist who has an answer for everything? You can't figure it out. You can't figure out other people, and you can't figure out yourself. And on top of all that, gradually feeling things, gradually coming to understand, that's what it means to be alive. That's enough to hold on to.

People toss out difficult questions so carelessly, so easily, as though they were nothing.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi today. Yesterday, too, there was so much I didn't understand. And you know what? It was a good day.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: A sturdy husband is precious, and "all right" is something to be grateful for.
item.description: The author reflects on the concept of "sturdiness"—being resilient and hard to break rather than simply strong—as an essential quality for navigating life, inspired by Miyazawa Kenji's poem about enduring hardship with a sturdy body.
item.content:encoded:
Lately, I've been thinking more and more about how important it is to be "sturdy." In Miyazawa Kenji's poem "Be Not Defeated by the Rain," there's a line about having a sturdy body. The poem seems to contain Kenji's vision of the ideal human being, and "sturdiness" was clearly a big part of that.

"Sturdy" is different from "strong." You can be sturdy without being strong. It's more like being hard to break, not easily falling ill, that sort of thing. If you think in terms of offense and defense, it's the latter. It means you don't fall apart. At first glance, that might not seem like much, but even strong things can be fragile, can break all the time. What I find appealing now is the idea of not being particularly strong, yet still not falling apart.

In "Be Not Defeated by the Rain," the person who lives being called a nobody by everyone, never praised, never a bother to anyone. The fact that this person is specifically described as having a sturdy body feels like something deeply important.

What did "sturdy" originally mean as a word, anyway? It's only now that I've gotten curious enough to look it up. The Japanese word is made of two characters: one meaning a unit of length, and the other meaning "husband" or "grown man." Put them together and, oh, I see. It's a full-grown man of good stature. A fine, fully-fledged man, apparently. So "sturdiness" refers to the qualities such a man would possess. I feel like I understand and don't understand at the same time, but, well, that's fine.

And then a particularly fine man would be "greatly sturdy," which is also the everyday word for "all right" or "no problem." So that's why "all right" means "all right"? Well, okay then. Starting to dig into the etymology actually made the nuance fly right out the window.

But what I originally wanted to say was simply this: I've come to realize, even at this late stage, that being sturdy matters. As long as you stay sturdy, you can probably stay all right.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. If you can manage to stay sturdy, you'll be ready for whatever comes next.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Sailing on the ship called "Hobonichi."
item.description: The author describes his organizational philosophy as a ship metaphor, where every crew member—regardless of rank or role—is essential and responsible for supporting one another to keep the vessel moving forward together.
item.content:encoded:
This goes back a long time. Probably about twenty-eight years ago. I realized that the way we worked could be likened to a ship.

Back then, organizations, companies, were thought of in terms of tree diagrams, tournament brackets, triangles with the all-important apex at the top. But what if you tipped that triangle on its side and laid it flat? Then there's no more top and bottom, just front and back. The whole thing starts to look like a ship, moving forward together. That was the image I had.

On a ship, every person aboard matters. Deciding which direction to go is important, and so is watching the weather, running the engine, loading and unloading cargo, cooking the meals, which is to say, peeling the potatoes. Everyone's role is essential, and you have to look out for one another or the whole thing falls apart. There's an old saying about how the ocean floor lies just on the other side of the hull. If the ship goes down, you all become flotsam.

I'm not trying to make everything sound life-or-death, but I liked the idea that we're all crew on the same ship, working together. Even if we happen to be on dry land. That's when I started using the word "crew."

It's not the most conventional way to put things, so I feel a little self-conscious about it and try not to overdo it. But when the moment feels right, I still say "crew." It's just "crew," after all.

Day to day, the crew members work in their own specialized areas. But when something comes up, they pitch in on things outside their expertise. As individual people, they do what they can to help each other. We're on a ship, so there's no other way, and mutual support is something everyone signed on for when they came aboard.

I think the people who joined Hobonichi recently and the ones who've been crew here for a long time probably share that same feeling. If you're on this ship, it's the same even for part-timers.

And come to think of it, at the "Life Is Fun" exhibition, I have a feeling the customers, the passengers, are on the same ship too.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. This ship weathered a typhoon yesterday. We're sailing on, and enjoying the voyage.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: I picked up a nice "thing I don't have to do."
item.description: The author argues that the most interesting and creative pursuits are those we choose freely rather than those we feel obligated to do, illustrating this insight through a chance discovery of a brilliant conversation about the liberating nature of voluntary activities.
item.content:encoded:
When I write, I almost never use quotations. I say "almost," so it's not that I never do, but I tend to avoid them. They're a hassle. Basically, I like to make do with whatever happens to be lying around. If I wanted to sound like an intellectual about it, I'd call it "something like bricolage." So when there's some impressive quote I could pull out, well, that's not exactly "making do with what's at hand," is it? It falls squarely into the category of things I'm bad at, things that feel like too much trouble. I was just about to write something along those lines when I stumbled upon a piece of text I was dying to quote. And what's more, it was just "lying around."

Sugano: "The things you have to do? You don't have to do them." (Repeats the same line three times.)

Nagata, who happened to be there: "Exactly. The things you have to do, there's only so interesting they can get."

Sugano: "Wait, what? Say that again, word for word."

Nagata: "The things you have to do, there's only so interesting they can get. But the things you don't have to do? There's no limit to how interesting those can be. Because, well, you don't have to do them."

What a magnificent conversation. It's from yesterday's live text coverage at the Hobonichi "Joys of Everyday Life" exhibition. If you find something like this just lying on the ground, of course you're going to pick it up. I'm swiping it. I'm not turning it in at the lost-and-found. I'm copying it down, just like that. Part quotation, part finders-keepers, part "something like bricolage."

Yes. The things you do out of obligation, because you feel like you have to, the outcome is predictable and it never gets interesting. The Hobonichi people who were having this conversation are out in Shinjuku again today, searching for things they don't have to do.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. How are things where you are after the typhoon? I wonder what's in store for all of us.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Soaking in a darkened bath with my eyes closed.
item.description: The essay explores how moments of vulnerability and defenselessness—like bathing with eyes closed or observing sleeping infants—provide essential psychological nourishment that helps us shed the constant tension of being guarded in daily life. The author suggests that regularly accessing this state of complete unguarded peace is as vital to our well-being as actual sleep itself.
item.content:encoded:
Whether you sleep well or not is beside the point. Just closing your eyes and lying down is enough, apparently. I've heard that plenty of times myself, but when someone else says it to you again, it brings the idea back fresh.

The "Life Enjoyment Market" got off to a really wonderful start. Everyone was busy in their own way, but all in all it was a calm, gentle day. Of course there were things that needed more thought, things we didn't handle well. That's inevitable, and I want to embrace all of it, the rough parts included. Still, the lack of sleep is something I know I'm not alone in, and that's a separate matter worth thinking about.

I soaked in a lukewarm bath, closed my eyes, and lay back. The bathroom light was off. It really does feel good. I wasn't asleep, but it was something very close. This must be a good thing, I thought. This is what matters.

And then, of course, I couldn't help myself. I started wondering what exactly makes it feel so good.

Being defenseless, maybe. That's what I think it is. Right. Most of the time, we're on guard. We do it because we have to, but it must put us under a tremendous amount of tension. So you create a moment where you can let that go, and you become completely unguarded. It really is quite similar to sleeping soundly, in total safety.

We human adults love to watch things that are completely, utterly defenseless. A baby, or a young animal, sleeping in the most perfect peace imaginable. When I think that we live in the same world as that seemingly infinite sense of safety, it makes me happy. All the worry, the anxiety, the suspicion, the combativeness, all of it fades away, and you feel like following those fragile little beings who've already gone ahead into that unguarded place.

That. That's what I need, properly, every single day. I have to take in that defenselessness like nourishment. Even just closing your eyes in the bathtub is a small step in the right direction.

Thank you for visiting Hobonichi again today. I'm writing this after getting out of the bath. Ooh, I'm sleepy.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: These days, age didn't matter, did it.
item.description: The essay celebrates a live exhibition event that brings together people of all ages and ranks who work collaboratively without hierarchy, creating an inclusive and energizing environment where everyone feels equally valued and welcomed.
item.content:encoded:
All right, it's time. The show is live. Live as in alive, as in living.

In a real, concrete place called the Shinjuku Sumitomo Building Triangle Plaza, real shops line up, real people gather, and the live event known as "Life's Little Pleasures Exhibition" begins. There's nothing virtual or digital about it. Well, of course digital tools are lending a hand, but everything that happens in that space is live.

The people who've been working pretty hard since the preparation stage were buzzing with excitement, praising each other. Yeah, I get it. The veterans say things like, "Maybe we've actually gotten good at this." Sure, everyone still has their worries, but somehow "everyone other than yourself" looks incredibly dependable. Well then, that's great. We open the doors feeling like we've done everything we could. What happens will happen. Let's keep this energy going.

Even before the event officially started, the live text coverage was already underway. Same as last time, a two-person team going at it full throttle. It only just hit me that those two are more than a generation apart in age. You'd never know it, though. One is senior, the other junior, but age has nothing to do with it.

And when you read the live text coverage, you can see the same thing in every single person who appears, moment by moment. It doesn't matter how old you are, young or old. This is something you really notice when you're doing something concrete like the Life's Little Pleasures Exhibition: the people at Hobonichi might not have ages at all. And no one lords it over anyone else for being higher or lower in the ranks. I thought to myself, genuinely, how comfortable that feels.

An old man like me, neither fussed over with concern nor pushed to the side, just included, welcomed into the fun. I'm truly grateful for that.

This time around, come to think of it, the core of the team running the Life's Little Pleasures Exhibition is made up of people in their twenties. Isn't that the best? I love a company like this.

And so, in good spirits, we're about to welcome Day One. Thank you for visiting Hobonichi today, too. I'm more than a little excited as I greet this day. Hobonichi is the best.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: The people you can see in the background of someone giving their all.
item.description: Our individual accomplishments are only possible through the constant support and labor of countless others, from the infrastructure that sustains us daily to the people who love and care for us. True success comes from recognizing this interdependence and feeling genuine gratitude for all those who enable our efforts.
item.content:encoded:
The phrase "it's all thanks to you" is a common one. It was even the title of a variety show, and you hear and read it all the time as a stock expression. But there are also plenty of times when someone says it and truly means it. In fact, come to think of it, I always mean it sincerely when I say it. Truly, from the bottom of my heart: it's all thanks to everyone.

No one can live without the help of others. When I was young, I often felt like I'd done everything myself. I won't entirely begrudge my younger self that kind of youthful pride, but at the same time, there's a part of me that wants to sit that younger me down and say, "That's not how it works." There was so much that happened thanks to other people, and I'm sure I caused plenty of trouble for them too. This isn't a lesson in morality. It's simply a matter of common sense.

Here's an easy example. A professional baseball manager, if he stepped up to the plate, couldn't hit the opposing pitcher's ball. He can't hit better than any of his players, and if he tried pitching or fielding, he'd be so hopeless he couldn't even make it into the game. No matter who you are, the things you can do on your own are few. Everything beyond that small sliver exists thanks to everyone else.

I mean, look, from the moment a baby comes crying into the world, it survives on nothing but the help of others. You can flush the toilet because someone built the sewage system. After your life is over, someone at the crematorium will take care of your remains. I could go on forever, but it's everything, absolutely everything!

Tomorrow, Monday, marks the start of a full week of "The Exhibition of Living Pleasantly." Behind every one of our teammates working as hard as they can, there are families and people who support them. While moms and dads are giving it their all, their children are waiting patiently at home, and their husbands and wives are holding things together. It's because of all those people that we're able to pour our sweat into this work. I'm genuinely, deeply grateful.

All right, it's the last day of preparation. Safety first, fun second. Let's go!

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today. The real work begins once you can see just how much you can't do on your own.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Flowers are still blooming.
item.description: While cherry blossoms dominate Japan's image, the essay celebrates the often-overlooked flowers that bloom throughout the year—azaleas, roses, plums, morning glories, and sunflowers—each beautiful and worthy of appreciation even without fanfare. The author reminds us that subtle beauty and quiet excellence exist all around us if we simply pay attention to what's in season.
item.content:encoded:
They say the flower of Japan is the cherry blossom. Whenever Japan comes up, it's always cherry blossoms, as if this were a country where nothing else blooms. And sure, cherry blossoms have that big-star quality about them. But listen.

After the cherry blossoms have scattered, down around knee height as you walk, azaleas burst into vivid color. There are so many of them, blooming everywhere, that nobody ever bothers to have an azalea-viewing party. Even kids today, just like kids from long ago, pluck the azalea flowers and sip the nectar from them. "So sweet!" they say, same as children always have.

Before long, in hedges and garden beds all over town, roses start to bloom. Roses are so popular, and flower shops carry them in every season, so it's hard to notice when they actually start blooming somewhere on the vine. But right around now is the perfect time for planted roses to flower. It's not just the magnificent blossoms worthy of a shop display. The modest, unremarkable ones are lovely too.

Right around the time you start thinking about the rainy season, the plum trees bear fruit. Oh, right, you remember. The Japanese word for the rainy season is written with the characters for "plum" and "rain." The plum blossoms themselves came back in the cold of February, didn't they? Then, depending on where you are, apricots start ripening too. I love apricots. This year I'll almost certainly make apricot jam myself again. Of all the jams in the world, apricot is my favorite.

By the end of the rainy season it's already summer, and all manner of weeds are stretching and spreading with wild abandon. They might be a nuisance, but I envy that kind of energy. And yes, morning glories send their tendrils climbing and open their flowers everywhere. Sunflowers stand tall in the city and the countryside alike. Being big is a perfectly fine thing to be known for, sunflowers.

They say the flower of Japan is the cherry blossom, but the flower calendar keeps going long after the blossoms fall. In every season, actors who may not be stars but who know their craft are blooming out there, whether we bother to look or not. Just as there is always music somewhere, flowers are blooming right now.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. And my sincere gratitude to everyone who tends the plantings along our streets.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Writing is hard enough as it is.
item.description: Writing, which has always been difficult for humans regardless of length or skill level, has become effortlessly achievable by AI—raising the profound question of why something so challenging for us comes so naturally to machines.
item.content:encoded:
No, wait, wait, wait. I just realized something incredible. We live our lives using Japanese, but when someone actually tells us to sit down and write something, more often than not we struggle with how hard it is. (Don't we?) If someone said, "Write about anything you want, around a thousand characters," what would you write, and how? You might think a hundred characters would be easier, but that's hard in its own way. Ten thousand characters? That's enormously, enormously difficult. A thousand, a hundred, a hundred thousand, or even just twenty: writing is hard no matter the length. And when you think about it, this has always been taken for granted. That's exactly why people who possessed the skill of putting things into words were called "good writers" or "authors" or "professionals," and could make a living from it.

From elementary school kids to full-grown adults to seasoned veterans of life, writing has always been a difficult thing. On top of that, writing something grammatically correct, logically consistent, free of typos and missing characters, and interesting on top of all that? I'd say eighty percent of the population would proudly declare, "Can't do it." I'd like to humbly include myself in that eighty percent, despite having spent all these years disguised as a professional and making a career of it.

I write on Hobonichi every single day. Today, same as always, I'm sitting here writing away like it's nothing. But I'm sure that anyone who actually specializes in writing would be furious at the sloppiness and haphazardness of what I keep putting out there for all to see. After all these years of writing, this is where I am. Am I an idiot?

So yes, writing has always been supposed to be hard. And yet AI can do it effortlessly. Without any practice at all, whether it's a thousand characters, a hundred thousand, a hundred, or twenty, it just keeps producing text. Just now, I tried asking it to "write something, anything," changing the specified length each time, running the experiment over and over. Long or short, every piece came out coherent, grammatically correct, and reasonably interesting, all in a matter of seconds.

How is it that this was so hard for us all along?

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. "A night where silence brings you closer than conversation." Written by AI. Touched up by Itoi.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: An old man buying tickets late at night. Me.
item.description: The author celebrates their lifelong habit of impulsively buying tickets to live performances, discovering through a spontaneous decision to see Sayuri Ishikawa that these spur-of-the-moment choices often lead to unforgettable experiences worth treasuring. They argue that acting on the urge to witness live events, especially now that it's easier than ever online, is always worthwhile because these moments can never be recreated once missed.
item.content:encoded:
We have the word "impulse buy," and I suppose this is a variety of that. I'm the kind of person who impulsively buys tickets. Even for events I'd never had any connection to, once I get the feeling that I really ought to see something, I want to go. Back before the internet existed, I was showing up at all sorts of performances and games. Murata Chōji's retirement game at Kawasaki Stadium. Misora Hibari's "Phoenix Concert" at the Tokyo Dome. Pink Floyd at the Hakone Aphrodite. The game where Sadaharu Oh hit home run number 756. RC Succession's debut at the Hibiya Open-Air Concert Hall. I'd get a sudden urge and head out on my own. I suppose you'd call it a rubbernecker's instinct. I have plenty more "I was there" stories to brag about, but I'll stop before I turn into an old man rambling about the past.

Still, this feeling of "I need to see this" has only grown healthier now that you can buy tickets online, because it's become so much easier to act on. I bought tickets to see Kiyoshi Maekawa late one night, all by myself, and went. He spotted me in the audience, and we had a reunion for the first time in twenty years.

Yesterday's "Sayuri Ishikawa Pre-55th Anniversary Concert" was another one of those tickets I clicked on in the middle of the night. Sayuri Ishikawa is apparently close friends with Akiko Yano, and it seems the two of them have gone to baseball games together in the States. On top of that, I once happened to be seated next to her at a Michiko Shimizu show, so we exchanged greetings. But that was about it.

At the end of the day, she's the voice behind this karaoke standard and that karaoke standard. She's someone who has given the world one "classic" of Japanese popular song after another. And yet, it occurred to me that never once having experienced Sayuri Ishikawa live was a real waste. So, striking while the iron was hot, I made the reservation, and headed to NHK Hall with Y-shita, who I'm not even all that close to.

Long story very short: the singing, incredible. The voice, I surrender. I was deeply grateful to the version of myself who had quietly bought those tickets in the middle of the night.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too. A live performance is something you can never get back once you've missed it. Impulse is the best.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title:
Source of flavor.
Wait, let me reconsider. "ソース味" refers to the flavor of Japanese Worcester-style sauce (ソース).
Sauce flavor.
item.description: As Japanese cuisine gains global popularity beyond traditional dishes like sushi, the author advocates for greater international appreciation of Japan's overlooked culinary gem: sauce-based dishes, which deserve far more recognition than they currently receive.
item.content:encoded:
When it came to deciding what to feed guests visiting from overseas, the standard answer used to be "sushi and tempura." The guests themselves had heard of these things, so they'd say, in halting Japanese, "I'd love to try that!" Nobody ever opened with "How about some natto?" But gradually, visitors started arriving better informed, and now things like ramen, onigiri, yakitori, and egg sandwiches have become quite popular too. Apparently, the quality of Japanese convenience store food was "discovered" during the Tokyo Olympics, held in the middle of the pandemic. And at some point, the rather formal phrase "guests from overseas" got replaced by the bouncier term "inbound," didn't it.

Every time news reaches us that some big Hollywood star was spotted eating ramen, it's funny how we find ourselves thinking, "Well, of course," feeling oddly proud even though we had nothing to do with making it. Lately I hear that tonkatsu restaurants are drawing plenty of inbound customers too. "Tonkatsu, naturally," I nod, as though I saw it coming.

Once things reach this point, you can't help wanting to guess what'll catch on next. I have a feeling it won't be natto. Tofu, made from the same soybeans, is already well known, and "edamame" has apparently entered the global vocabulary. But natto... that's going to be a tough sell.

Since predictions are futile, let me just go with personal preference. What I'd really like to champion is the vast universe of sauce flavor. Sauce already plays a supporting role alongside tonkatsu and the rest of the fried-food team, but I don't think it's getting nearly enough time in the spotlight. Yakisoba with sauce, not the Chinese-style kind, is wonderful, and the whole appeal of "konamon," those flour-based street foods, comes down to that sauce flavor, if you ask me.

So the next time you're eating out with a friend from abroad, casually steer them toward something with sauce. Don't worry about what good might come of it. Just do it.

Thanks for visiting Hobonichi again today. You know, the range of flavors and aromas in sauce is even more diverse than what you'll find in wine.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: The form of manzai, and fiction.
item.description: Manzai comedy's partnership format allows for bold, transgressive humor that self-corrects through its duo dynamic, whereas solo performance and especially written fiction lack this built-in safeguard—yet fiction's solitary nature is precisely what gives it the freedom to explore the full complexity of human experience without constraint.
item.content:encoded:
The format of manzai comedy holds a real advantage in our current age, and that's because you have a partner right there beside you to play the straight man. This means the one playing the fool can say outrageous things, stupid things, irresponsible things, even dangerous things. Because their teammate is right there to say, "No way!" and pull them back to common sense. Even if one half of the duo says something wrong, the team as a whole can claim it still falls within the rules of society. In a time when criticism and opposing views fly in from every conceivable angle, this may be the most survivable form of expression there is.

If a solo performer stood at the microphone and said the same things, those words would be taken as "his opinion," plain and simple, and sometimes held against him as "his mistake." But when you really think about it, comedic talk was never meant to be a statement of personal views. It was supposed to be a craft, an art of making people laugh. And yet, because it overlaps with the speaker's own voice, people are quick to assume, "So that's what this comedian actually thinks." But if you only ever say things that stay safely within the bounds of common sense, you'll never produce a single laugh. In that sense, the manzai format, with its built-in self-criticism, is perfectly suited to an era like ours.

Writing, however, is not a team act. When you say something on the page, it belongs to you alone. You can't have someone jumping in beside you to correct you as you go. Well, you could try, but writing while preemptively apologizing for every possible objection would be so constrained as to be unbearable. And that, I think, is exactly why fiction is so interesting. Characters in a novel are free to transgress. You can give voice to evil, to hypocrisy, to madness. This is a possibility we ought to open up even further.

Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. Come to think of it, isn't it true that so much of what we know about being human, we first learned from novels?
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: It might not be worth it, and yet.
item.description: The essay celebrates how artists and creative professionals—whether filmmakers or musicians—pursue their passion beyond financial considerations, and argues that this commitment to excellence beyond profit is essential to human culture and what gives life genuine meaning.
item.content:encoded:
The Korean Netflix drama "Everyone Is Fighting Their Worthless Self" is finally reaching its conclusion with Episode 12. As I write this, I've only watched up to Episode 11, and I honestly wonder whether they can wrap everything up in just one more episode (about an hour and five minutes, I think). Maybe it'll end with a feeling of "and the story goes on." Either way, this drama truly pulled me in. I imagine there are plenty of people who haven't seen it yet, so I won't say too much, but I think Park Hye-young's screenplay is simply outstanding. Part of what drew me in may be that the story is about people who make films. These are people who pour fierce passion into filmmaking, living their lives with their individual dreams and emotions all tangled up together. Of course they want a hit, and of course they want commercial success. But without that something more, that passion and seriousness beyond just business, this story wouldn't have worked, and that's exactly what makes it worth watching.

There's only so much you can accomplish when all you think about is profit and loss. When you reduce everything to labor and compensation, there's a whole world of things you simply can't do. So why do these characters throw themselves so completely into something that doesn't even pay off? The show never gives you a direct answer. But without people like them, it's not just film. Every form of expression would stop being refined, would stop surviving into the future.

Come to think of it, Kenji Ozawa recently said about his current live performances, "Playing with young musicians is really stimulating." I thought, yes, of course it is, and at the same time I found myself imagining how even now, in this day and age, there are young people practicing music intensely, choosing performance as their profession. In a way, if you start talking about cost-effectiveness, playing an instrument at that level is a job that just doesn't add up. But it made me happy to know that these "young people" who inspire someone like Ozawa are still out there, still growing. The fact that so many people still enter professions that demand enormous skill and relentless practice, going beyond calculations of profit and loss, beyond labor and compensation, that really is something I'd call "hope."

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. Being able to do what you truly want to do is an extraordinary kind of joy, after all.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Isn't there anyone who looks happy?
item.description: The author reflects on the paradox that successful people often don't appear genuinely happy, prompting them to consider interviewing individuals whom others perceive as happy to understand what happiness actually looks like in real life.
item.content:encoded:
Is there someone you look at and think, "That person seems so happy"? It could be someone close to you, or even yourself, or some famous person you've never met. Better if they're not fictional, though. And ideally, someone you could actually go and see if you wanted to. What I'm saying is, I'd love to meet one of those people, someone others look at and think, "They seem so happy," and ask them all sorts of things. I haven't worked out any of the details yet. It just came to me.

Yesterday I went to see *The Devil Wears Prada 2*. The movie was good, really entertaining, and clearly a hit. The original, now over twenty years old, is still fun to watch, and pulling off a sequel with the same cast is practically an acrobatic feat. The fact that they managed to put together a film this solid is genuinely impressive. It's not all glamour and lightness, but the dazzling, stylish costumes do their work beautifully.

On the walk home, I was thinking to myself. Why is it that this movie doesn't feel like a happy ending? Every character is trying hard, or being smart, but to me, none of them looked happy. Including the main character. They win, they succeed, and yet there wasn't a single person on screen who struck me as someone I'd describe as "happy."

So then I turned the question on myself. The people I look at and think, "They seem so happy," where are they? Or the people everyone would point to and say, "Now there's someone who's happy," who are they, and where are they living? Do we think that about presidents, or pop stars, or athletes? Among the people I actually know, who would I say that about?

Happiness is subjective in the first place, I suppose, and the more I think about it the more tangled everything gets. But if that's the case, what if I found people who others perceive as happy, and sat down and talked to them about all kinds of things? It's still just an idea I've had on my own, but I'm planning to bring it to everyone and see what they think.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today. I wonder how many treatises on happiness have been written over the course of human history.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Remembering that I was once a child too.
item.description: The essay reflects on how children's peculiar thoughts, innocent misconceptions, and forgotten moments—from imagining baseball scouts to discovering false "laws of nature"—subtly shape our personalities and persist in our memories well into adulthood. These childhood experiences, whether true or false, become part of who we are and how we think, revealing that children are ultimately the raw material from which adults are formed.
item.content:encoded:
Children think strange thoughts. And those strange thoughts, in their own way, end up nourishing something deep inside you later on. I might as well be honest about it now: I was genuinely alert to the possibility that a professional baseball scout, especially one from the Yomiuri Giants, might be watching me. I was particularly vigilant when I stood on the riverbank of the Tone River, throwing stones at the bridge pylons (ostensibly as pitching practice). When something "isn't entirely impossible, when the probability isn't zero," a child's conclusion is: it could happen.

I used to draw on hard-packed ground with wax crayons quite often. If you kept stubbornly filling in a large enough area, the surface of the earth would become slick and smooth, almost as if it had been waterproofed. One rainy day, I was doing just that when I made a "discovery": the rain didn't land on the slippery parts. I had no idea how to articulate this epoch-making law of nature, so I never told anyone. It's been about seventy years now, and I still haven't. Perhaps because I've kept this secret for so long, my thinking is occasionally infiltrated by a kind of inverted logic, along the lines of "a waterproof surface repels rain." Things like "bulletproof vests don't get hit by bullets." Grand, spectacularly wrong ideas. I consider it a personality trait.

Incidentally, I discovered you can buy wax crayons through a certain jungle-themed online retailer, so I ordered some right away. I'm thinking of giving them to my daughter's daughter, but she probably doesn't want them.

I loved books about inventions and was perpetually ready to invent something myself. In third grade, a classmate won a prize at an invention contest. It was obvious his father had made the entry. I was frustrated, but I couldn't say anything about it. Nor did I channel that frustration into working harder. And yet, for all that, I've remembered it ever since. Haven't forgotten it at all. Children, I think now, are the raw material of adults.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi again today. The lies you told as a child, you remember those for a surprisingly long time, too.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Peace without words.
item.description: The author reflects on the simple beauty of watching creatures—dogs and babies—naturally gather and coexist without language or purpose, and finds in their wordless presence a poignant counterpoint to a life spent working with words.
item.content:encoded:
You've probably seen this kind of scene before, at a daycare, a nursery, a park. Tiny children, barely more than babies, playing together, looking happy. Well, they're not always getting along, and sometimes they seem to be ignoring each other entirely. So maybe "playing together" is a stretch. Oh, and dogs at a dog park are the same way. Some are chasing each other around, some are off on their own, some are tumbling together. It's all sorts of things. But either way, in a space that isn't all that big, there's this scene of creatures naturally gathered together. Nobody's in charge. There's no apparent purpose. And there are no "words" to speak of (well, okay, maybe some babbling, some barking). Each of them has their own hunger, their own sleepiness, their own little quarrels. They're all separate individuals, separate dogs. And yet, somehow, they're just naturally there together.

I like watching that kind of thing. And maybe it's not just me. Maybe humans in general like watching it.

I know perfectly well that a world with purpose, with systems and rules, with scheming and strategy and tactics, can be fascinating as a game. That's why I watch dramas, why I watch sports. But there's something about "living creatures, simply being there together" that is its own kind of wonderful, its own kind of quietly heartwarming. I even feel something like longing when I see it.

Especially the part about having no "words." I envy that. I'm someone who has spent his life working with words, and I should know better than anyone how powerful and precious they are. And this very piece I'm writing right now is made of words. But maybe that's exactly why, maybe it's precisely because of that, that I find myself longing for silence.

Dogs and babies can speak with their eyes alone. Sometimes they leap at you, or climb into your arms. These wordless creatures are unbearably dear to me.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. Words can be weapons, and they can be the red thread of fate that binds us. But so can silence.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: It's not really a "culture festival," you know.
item.description: The Hobonichi Life & Enjoyment Exhibition creates a unique collective experience that transcends typical work—one where shared effort, unspoken understanding, and post-event reflection generate both immediate fulfillment and hope for future iterations.
item.content:encoded:
At last, the Life & Enjoyment Exhibition is almost here. There's a certain tension running through the Hobonichi office. Everyone's shifted up a gear in their own way. Of course, the exhibition is work, but it also feels something like a company trip where you actually work. Then again, put it that way and people might think, "That sounds awful." And sure, a company trip where you're forced to work would be the worst. I can't quite put it right. People often compare it to a school festival, but "school festival" doesn't feel like enough. There's unevenness, sure, and some things are done better than others, but what we're doing really is work. Maybe the closest way to describe it is "a place where we go through something together." The crew here at the office, the people exhibiting, the people who come as visitors, the people who show up to help out part-time: everyone goes through it together.

What exactly are we going through? Well, that's the part I can't put into words. It's one of those feelings that resists the kind of "putting it into language" that's so fashionable these days. Each person feels it, and we exchange it, share it. Then, afterward, everyone keeps talking it over, as memories, as reflections, as words, as methods. That turns into the wisdom of "next time, let's try it this way." And the feeling that next time we can do even more becomes something like hope. When it's over, we share the exhaustion too, and rest. You find yourself itching to talk to people about all of it. Then you go back to your regular post, to work that isn't the exhibition. Plans for the next one start quietly taking shape, and the cycle toward the future begins.

Michael Polanyi's idea of "tacit knowledge" was developed by Professor Nonaka Ikujiro into the SECI model, and what we're doing might be close to that. Though it could also be that we're all just kicking around a ball labeled "I can't explain it well, but it's good," and having fun doing it. We're not running things according to theory, so here we are before the big event, bustling around as usual.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. I'm getting myself ready to go the full distance through that long stretch, in my own way.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: A place where I can see the one I love.
item.description: People naturally gather in places centered around what they love, whether it's a supermarket opening in 1979 or a modern community space—the fundamental human desire to connect with others who share your passions remains unchanged across decades.
item.content:encoded:
The place where you can meet the people you love is the place those people love. So if you can figure out what someone loves, you can figure out where to find them. Sometimes people who share the same interests meet as strangers and end up finding someone dear to them.

It sounds like I'm laying out the laws of matchmaking for the internet age, but actually, that opening line, "The place where you can meet the people you love," comes from a jingle I wrote when I was around thirty. It came back to me out of nowhere, and I found myself humming it.

It was 1979. A Seiyu supermarket was opening in front of the train station in Saga, and this was the theme for the grand opening campaign. I went to Saga for location scouting and did some interviews, but everyone there just kept saying, "There's nothing here." From our perspective, we kept thinking, "But it's right in front of the station!" And sure enough, there really were very few people around. "Sometimes a biker gang loops around near the station," they told us. But plenty of people also said, "Once the Seiyu opens, I think folks will start coming, little by little." So there was hope.

If "something" (in this case, a Seiyu) appears in a place where people think there's "nothing," people will gather. Well, that's exactly what the company must have been thinking when they decided to build it. That was more or less my line of reasoning when I wrote the copy. If I remember right, it was: "The place where you can meet the people you love." And that became the lyrics for the opening of the jingle.

I can recall what I was thinking nearly fifty years ago as if it were just the other day. And I realize I'm still thinking about the same kinds of things now. It really doesn't change, does it, the stuff people do.

The place where you can meet the people you love is the place those people love. Everything Hobonichi has been doing all along, the recently opened "Hobo no Eki AKAGI," the "Exhibition of the Joys of Everyday Life" that's about to start. It's all the same thing. It's nothing as fancy as "community building" or "regional revitalization." It's just that people who love the same things want to gather together. That's all.

Thank you for coming to Hobonichi today, too. Incidentally, that Saga Seiyu closed in 2018.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: Everyone has more energy than they know what to do with.
item.description: Many people wait for clear instructions or goals from external sources to perform at their best, but in an increasingly complex world where routine tasks are automated, true fulfillment comes from the creative work of defining your own purpose rather than simply executing someone else's vision. The real challenge and gift of modern life is discovering what you genuinely want to do, rather than waiting for someone to tell you what needs to be done.
item.content:encoded:
It's possible that a great many people are thinking something like this: "If someone would just tell me clearly, 'Do this,' if someone would just give me specific instructions on what I'm supposed to do, I could really show what I'm capable of."

They might also be thinking, "If someone would just present me with a goal, a bright future to work toward, I'd give it everything I've got. I'd work myself to the bone."

And I suspect that's probably true. I think there are an enormous number of people who believe, "The reason I can't perform at my full potential is that nobody's given me something I should be doing, and I haven't found anything I want to do."

In dramas, there's often a leader you can look up to, and the story revolves around the team rallying together to help him succeed. You see it in sports stories, war stories, corporate sagas. The team members in those stories already know what needs to be done. So no matter how hard it gets, no matter how impossible it seems, no matter what sacrifices are required, all they have to do is throw themselves into it with everything they've got.

I imagine some people can't help saying things like, "If I were in that kind of situation, I could get serious too," or "It's because the people at the top don't have a clear vision." I get it. It feels like you've got all this untapped energy just waiting to be unleashed.

But here's the thing. Situations where you know exactly what needs to be done and all that's left is to give it your all, those situations are becoming rarer and rarer in this day and age, whatever it might have been like in the past.

And if what needs to be done is already clear, isn't that exactly the kind of thing AI will end up handling instead of humans?

You have the ladder. You have the strength to climb it. But you don't know where to lean it or where it's supposed to take you. In times like these, actually having something you want to do, something you feel you should do, that's an incredible gift. Maybe creativity is precisely this: making the situation where you can bring your strength to bear.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi again today. First, ask yourself what you want. Even if it's hazy, just try looking at it.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: A personal essay: the deliciousness of rice
item.description: The key to delicious rice is removing moisture—whether through the active fanning process of sushi rice or by avoiding the "keep warm" setting that traps steam and makes rice soggy—and understanding that freshly cooked warmth is fleeting, so cold rice is often the better choice.
item.content:encoded:
I won't say every single one, but most sushi rice is delicious, wouldn't you agree? The seasoning of vinegar and a little sugar is part of why, of course, but the grains themselves are delicious too. Each individual grain is smooth and glossy, existing independently. You can really tell that rice is what it is: the seed of a grass, a plant called paddy.

Sushi rice is made by coating freshly cooked rice with seasoned vinegar. And what's crucial in this coating process is the fanning, with a flat fan or the like, to cool the rice down. You're lowering the temperature and driving off the steam. By doing this, the surface moisture is whisked away, and the grains stop getting waterlogged.

I know that was a rather tedious explanation, but I want you to understand this first. The opposite of sushi rice, where cooling and removing moisture makes it delicious, is what happens with "keep warm" mode. While cooked rice sits on the warmer, the steam has nowhere to escape. That steam keeps touching the surface of every grain. Naturally, the grains turn soft and soggy. That's the problem, right there. I want you to feel it. I certainly did.

There are all kinds of theories and methods when it comes to cooking rice. I've tried plenty of them myself, but lately, if I put it as simply as possible, I've arrived at this conclusion: "As long as you don't keep it on warm, it's going to taste pretty good." Transferring it to a wooden rice container is great. Even a non-wooden one works surprisingly well. Shaping it into rice balls is good too. Or after you've stirred the freshly cooked rice, you can just leave the lid off and drape a paper towel over it. You could even scoop it into a bowl and let it cool down that way.

The pleasure of "warm rice" belongs to the moment it's freshly cooked, and that moment only. If you're going to let time pass, skip the warmer and just eat it cold. If you want it hot again, the microwave is a better bet.

I realize you might be wondering what I'm getting so worked up about, but I ended up writing here the kind of thing I'd normally just say to a friend. Thank you, as always, for visiting Hobonichi today. Think about it: sushi and onigiri are delicious even when they're cold. Of course they are.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: People I admire, who are incredibly resilient.
item.description: The essay argues that what truly matters in extraordinary people—whether performers, leaders, or artists—is not their polished output but their underlying physical and mental stamina, the fundamental "root force" that makes everything else possible. The author reflects on his own lack of such energy while appreciating those who possess it abundantly.
item.content:encoded:
There are things that sound completely blunt and obvious, yet are fundamentally, incredibly important. For example, something I've written about here a few times: when Takaaki Yoshimoto spoke about why he became interested in Shinran, he said it was largely because he grew up in a household deeply devoted to Jodo Shinshu Buddhism. Or when someone asked him, "Why do you always have the TV on?" and he answered, "Probably because I get lonely." These aren't the kind of impressive-sounding answers people expect, so an interviewer might feel let down. But they get at something truly fundamental. And what I want to say is very much in that same vein.

I saw Kenji Ozawa's incredibly powerful live show, and all I can talk about is the sheer energy of it. I understand what he's going for, what he wants people to feel. But if you think of it in terms of a tree, all of that belongs to the flowers and the fruit. And I'm all for everyone admiring and discussing how magnificent those are. But what I kept sensing was the root force, the fundamental power, root and trunk, that makes those flowers and fruit possible. That's all I wanted to talk about. Bluntly, with no regard for politeness or decorum, what I thought was: "That guy is ridiculously tough!"

He looks like he might be some delicate, sensitive young gentleman, but really he's storing up an almost ferocious amount of energy. It reminds me, I think Seiji Ozawa must have been the same way, just brimming with energy. All the truly extraordinary people in the world have incredible physical stamina. I bet that's true of politicians too, isn't it? And in cases where someone doesn't have that natural stamina, they grind themselves down to nothing, pour out every last ounce of abnormal energy, and essentially burn through their whole life doing it.

Yesterday I went to see Reiwa Roman's big live show, and the energy they put behind every laugh was like a weapon going off. People who can do that kind of thing are the ones who draw others to them. And me? I'm frail, low-energy, and honestly kind of pathetic.

Thanks for coming to Hobonichi again today. I'd like to find a way to keep things interesting, even if I stay pathetic.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi

item.title: The lion chases the rabbit with all its might.
item.description: The essay celebrates how Natsume Soseki approached the humble subject of learning to ride a bicycle with the same earnest craftsmanship and literary devotion that defined his major works, demonstrating that great writers apply their full artistry to every task, no matter how mundane.
item.content:encoded:
I'll begin with a quotation. This may be the first time I've ever done something like this. The source is Aozora Bunko, so you can read the full text there if you like. (I've put the furigana readings in parentheses and added a few line breaks.)

"In the autumn of the year 1902 of the Western calendar, on a forgotten day of a forgotten month, I hoisted a white flag from my bedroom window and sued for peace with the landlady of my boarding house. No sooner had I done so than the old woman set about hauling her one-hundred-and-sixty-pound frame up to the very top of the third floor. I say 'set about hauling' rather than 'hauled' in order to convey just how much effort was involved. The staircase comprised no fewer than forty-two steps. She paused to rest twice along the way. After an expenditure of three minutes and five seconds, the face of this magnificent woman, which ought to have worn an expression of triumph, appeared with agonized abruptness in my doorway. The surroundings shrank to nothing beside her. Addressing myself, who bore the honor of this audience upon my two narrow and burdened shoulders, the old woman, as the first article of our peace terms, issued the following command: Ride a bicycle."

I can't quite work out his system for punctuation, and the writing looks intimidating at first glance, but every line is plainly angling for a laugh. This is the opening of Natsume Soseki's essay "The Bicycle Diary." He blends a grandiose literary style with the patter of a comic storyteller, as if to say: savor my literary craftsmanship until you can't stand it anymore. And that tone carries on, unbroken, for the entire piece. What he's really writing about is simply this: he had to learn to ride a bicycle, and no matter what he tried it was hopeless and he hated every minute of it. Yet somehow the whole thing is rendered like the ornamental transom carving in a newly built house, executed with all the painstaking finesse of a master artisan.

After all, this is Natsume Soseki we're talking about. A great man. His face was on our money. A towering figure of Meiji literature. You might imagine he could dash off something like this with his left hand, scribbling it out like idle doodling, half asleep the whole time.

Yeah, no. I don't think so. He was writing in earnest, moment by moment, with all the seriousness that includes play and pleasure and devotion to craft.

Well, it's not as though I'm reflecting on my own shortcomings here or anything.

Thank you, as always, for coming to Hobonichi today. Soseki wrote this while studying in England at the age of thirty-five. Me? I can ride a bicycle just fine.
item.author: Shigesato Itoi
